questions.scenario: Your backup script needs a timestamp in the filename. Hard-coding dates is not an option.
questions.question: Store the current date (format: YYYY-MM-DD) in a variable called TODAY and print it.
questions.description: $(cmd) captures the stdout of a command and substitutes it inline. Trailing newlines are stripped automatically. This is the modern syntax — backticks `cmd` are legacy and should be avoided.
use_cases: Capture hostname, user, or system info at runtime
Store command output for later use in the script
hints: Use $(date ...) to capture the date command output
The format flag for date is +%Y-%m-%d

questions.scenario: You need to count the number of lines in /home/student/logs/app.log and include the count in a message.
questions.question: Print the message 'Log has X lines' where X is the actual line count from wc -l.
questions.description: Command substitution works inline inside double-quoted strings. The result is word-split and glob-expanded unless quoted — always quote: "$(cmd)" to be safe.
use_cases: Include command output in log messages
hints: Use $(wc -l < file) inside the echo string

questions.scenario: You need to build a filename using today's date AND the current user, nested into one string.
questions.question: Create a filename string in the format 'backup-USER-DATE.sql' using nested command substitutions.
questions.description: $() can be nested: $(outer $(inner)). The inner substitution runs first. Backticks cannot be nested cleanly — another reason to always use $(). Nested substitutions are common in dynamic filename generation.
use_cases: Generate unique filenames per user per date
Build complex strings from multiple runtime values
Create archive names that include environment information
hints: Nest two $() calls inside a string
$(whoami) gets the user, $(date +%Y%m%d) gets the date

questions.scenario: A script captures output of 'echo  hello   world  ' (extra spaces). Show that unquoted substitution splits it.
questions.question: Demonstrate word splitting: store 'echo  hello   world  ' in VAR unquoted and pass it to echo, vs quoted.
questions.description: Unquoted $var is word-split by bash on IFS (default: space/tab/newline) and glob-expanded. This destroys spacing and can trigger glob matches. Always quote: echo "$VAR" not echo $VAR.
use_cases: Understand why scripts break with filenames containing spaces
Know when quoting is required vs optional
hints: VAR=$(echo '  hello   world  '), then try echo $VAR vs echo "$VAR"
Run: VAR=$(echo '  hello   world  '); echo $VAR; echo "---"; echo "$VAR"

validation.command: VAR=$(echo '  hello   world  '); echo $VAR; echo '---'; echo "$VAR"

questions.scenario: You want the number of currently running processes. Use command substitution to get the count.
questions.question: Store the number of running processes (from ps aux | wc -l) in PROC_COUNT and print it.
questions.description: Command substitution is the primary way to capture numeric output for use in scripts. Combined with arithmetic expansion, this lets you compute and compare values at runtime.
use_cases: Alert if process count exceeds a threshold
Validate expected process counts in health checks
hints: PROC_COUNT=$(ps aux | wc -l) captures the count
